trajectory_summary: The call starts cold — Carl arrives with pre-formed second thoughts and two prepared objections — and Carter's intro failure (no legitimacy re-establishment, immediate logistics framing) gives Carl no reason to re-engage before the objections land. Carter spends the first 90 seconds in scope creep, answering roof-damage and warranty questions that belong to the closer. The call recovers when Carter redirects to the design report at 01:56, which is the correct setter move and the pivot that keeps Carl on the line. The booking at 04:45 is real but structurally thin: no button-up anchors (no computer requirement, no 'is that fair' handshake, no one-hour block stated), the bill is not yet in hand, and the appointment is 3-4 days out in a holiday week. Carter's self-correction at 03:52 — pivoting to book rather than ending on a cold callback — deserves credit as the move that salvaged a sit from a call that was trending toward a ghost.
outcome_dispute: The call ends with a tentative Saturday booking, but the appointment is structurally fragile: button-up protocol was not completed (no computer/no-driving anchor, no 'is that fair' handshake, no one-hour block stated), the utility bill has not been received, and Carl is still driving during the close. If this is a paid lead, the bill is required before the appointment can be set per codex — but lead type is not confirmed for this call, so that condition cannot be stated as certain. The fragile-booking classification stands on the button-up gaps alone.

primary_coaching_focus.quote: I'll be super quick. It looks like you talked to us a few days back. Looks like we just need a utility bill and a time to come by and show you a solar design. Is that right?
primary_coaching_focus.why: Carter's logistics-first opening is the structural cause of the entire objection cascade that follows. Carl arrived with second thoughts already formed — the intro was the one moment to re-establish legitimacy and re-engage his original motivation before those objections landed. Carter skipped it entirely. This is the highest-leverage coaching beat on this call because fixing it changes the trajectory of every returning-contact call Carter handles, not just this one.

key_moments.quote: I'll be super quick. It looks like you talked to us a few days back. Looks like we just need a utility bill and a time to come by and show you a solar design. Is that right?
key_moments.stewart_read: Carter skips the intro beat entirely — no legitimacy re-establishment, no company framing, no pause for re-engagement. He treats the prior conversation as having done all the trust work and opens directly into logistics. For a customer who has since talked himself into 'second thoughts,' this is the worst possible opening: it confirms the call is transactional, not consultative, and gives Carl no reason to re-engage before his pre-formed objections surface. The cold starting state at 00:28 is a direct consequence of this intro failure.

key_moments.quote: Yeah. I mean, it it wouldn't damage it at all.
key_moments.stewart_read: Carter answers Carl's roof-damage concern as a technical question, engaging in closer-tier content rather than redirecting to the specialist. The codex's universal escape hatch — 'That's exactly the kind of question our specialist is built to answer' — was available here. Instead, Carter validates the concern as a debate topic, which invites the follow-up escalation at 00:53 and 01:09.

key_moments.quote: How long is the warranty? Because my roof has a lifetime warranty.
key_moments.stewart_read: By the third exchange in the roof-concern sequence, Carter is three levels deep in closer-tier content — damage, mounting, and now warranty duration. He gives a specific number ('twenty five years') and a vague reassurance. This is the compounding cost of the first scope-creep entry at 00:48: each technical answer invites a more specific follow-up. The multi-touch pattern shows this is not a single slip but a sustained drift.

key_moments.quote: I just don't wanna send you guys on a wild goose chase because, you know, I I think it's gonna be very limited in what we can do.
key_moments.stewart_read: This is a guilt-as-objection / soft refusal. Carl is not expressing genuine concern about Ion's time — he's giving Carter a polite exit ramp while managing his own discomfort about proceeding. The codex flags this pattern: 'I don't want to waste your time' is a soft refusal that should be reframed as customer-in-control. Carter's response ('we meet with people all day every day, so it's no issues') is a reasonable deflection but misses the reframe — he reassures Ion rather than returning agency to Carl.
